title: Triple vs ultrawide FOV: how much of the corner you actually see
shows: A top-down field-of-view diagram comparing a single flat/curved super-ultrawide monitor against a three-panel angled triple setup, both drawn around the same driver head position. The point is peripheral vision: the flat ultrawide sits in one plane roughly in front of you, while angled side screens on a triple wrap into your peripheral vision so the apex and the car alongside stay on-screen instead of off the edge.
dataPoints: Driver head at center as the FOV origin point
Single 49" 32:9 ultrawide (5120x1440): one gently curved plane, ~55-65 degrees horizontal coverage of vision
Single 57" 32:9 ultrawide (7680x2160): wider plane but still a single flat-ish arc in front, still no side-window geometry
Triple 27" panels angled ~55-60 degrees inward: center screen straight ahead, two side panels wrapped into peripheral vision
Triple wrap reaches ~180-195 degrees of horizontal vision (matches NLR stand's stated 195 degrees)
Callout: the ultrawide shows a WIDER single image; the triple shows you the SIDE of the track (mirrors, apex, car alongside) that an ultrawide crops off
Callout: triples require in-sim triple-projection/bezel correction so the angled side views render at the correct perspective

title: Resolution vs GPU load: the pixels each display asks your card to push
shows: A bar/tier comparison of total rendered pixel count for the main display choices, mapped to the NVIDIA GPU tier the sim-racing community actually recommends to hold a stable frame rate at each. The takeaway is that the display decides the GPU, and a triple-1440p or 57" Dual-4K setup is a materially bigger ask than a 49" DQHD ultrawide.
dataPoints: 49" ultrawide DQHD (5120x1440) = ~7.4 million pixels — comfortable on an RTX 5070 (~$550-650)
Triple 1440p (3x 2560x1440) = ~11.1 million pixels — wants an RTX 5080 (~$1,000-1,200) to hold 120Hz on a full grid
57" Neo G9 Dual 4K (7680x2160) = ~16.6 million pixels — the heaviest flat-panel load, RTX 5080/5090 territory + DisplayPort 2.1 to drive 240Hz
Triple 4K (3x 3840x2160) = ~24.9 million pixels — 5090-only, shown as the 'don't unless you must' extreme
Annotation: triples also pay a multi-view rendering tax (3 camera passes) beyond raw pixel count
Annotation: the RTX 5070 is the value baseline, the RTX 5080 is the triple-1440p/high-res sweet spot (community-cited)
